section: 2. DEVELOPMENT OF THE METAL TRANSFER PROCESS
text: The current experiments utilize mechanically agitated contactors as an alternative to packed columns for the metal transfer process. $^{1,2}$ The Lewis-type contactor appears to have the potential for achieving acceptable rare-earth mass transfer rates with minimum dispersal of the salt and bismuth phases. $^{3,4}$ This is an important factor, since entrainment of bismuth into processed fuel salt that is returned to the reactor cannot be tolerated.

section: 2. DEVELOPMENT OF THE METAL TRANSFER PROCESS
text: An engineering experiment (MTE-3) in which the salt flow rate was about $1\%$ of that required for processing the fuel salt from a 1000 MW(e) MSBR was operated during 1972 to measure rare-earth mass transfer rates across the salt-bismuth interfaces.

section: 2.3 Design of the Metal Transfer Process Facility
text: Design of the metal transfer process facility (MTPF) in which the fourth metal transfer experiment (MTE-4) will be carried out was underway when the MSR program was terminated in 1973.6 Briefly reviewed, MTE-4 is an engineering experiment that will use salt flow rates that are 5 to $10\%$ of those required for processing a 1000-MW(e) MSBR.

section: Ornl Dwg 74-10073
text: Figure 9 shows a comparison of the measured values of mass transfer coefficient with the Lewis correlation.

section: Ornl Dwg 74-6331R3
text: We believe that the initiation of salt entrainment into the bismuth begins to occur at a stirrer speed between 160 and 180 rpm.

section: Ornl Dwg 74-6331R3
text: 9 seems to indicate, the mass transfer coefficients in the salt-bismuth system are lower by a factor of about 0.35 than predicted by the Lewis correlation, this corrected correlation could be used to estimate the area and bismuth flow rate required in the rare-earth removal contactor in the present flowsheet.

section: Ornl Dwg 74-6331R3
text: The Lewis correlation predicts that mass transfer coefficients are approximately proportional to the agitator diameter, $d$ , to the 3.30 power.

section: Ornl Dwg 74-6331R3
text: Since it has been shown[15] that the allowable agitator speed below which there is no phase dispersal is inversely proportional to the 1.43 power of the agitator diameter, at speeds slightly below the limiting agitator speed the mass transfer coefficient will apparently be proportional to the agitator diameter to the 0.94 power.

section: Ornl Dwg 74-6331R3
text: These results were used to estimate the area required for a single-stage contactor and the bismuth flow rate to remove cerium, which has the shortest removal time (16.6 days) of the rare earths in the

section: Ornl Dwg 74-6331R3
text: For a salt flow rate of 0.88 gpm (corresponding to the 10-day cycle time used in the reference process flowsheet) and a distribution coefficient of 0.062 (the distribution coefficient that was used in the reference processing flowsheet), an area for mass transfer of $10\mathrm{ft}^2$ will allow removal of cerium on a 16.6-day cycle if the bismuth flow rate through the contactor is 30 gpm and a 3-ft-diam agitator is used.

section: Ornl Dwg 74-6331R3
text: This bismuth flow rate is only about 2-1/2 times the flow rate specified in the processing plant flowsheet on the basis of equilibrium calculations.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The reference flowsheet for processing the fuel salt from a molten-salt breeder reactor (MSBR) is based upon removal of uranium by fluorination to $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ as the first processing step.[16] The uranium removed in this step must subsequently be returned to the fuel salt stream before it returns to the reactor.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The method for recombining the uranium with the fuel carrier salt (reconstituting the fuel salt) is to absorb gaseous $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ into a recycled fuel salt stream containing dissolved $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ by utilizing the reaction:

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The resultant $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{5}$ would be reduced to $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ with hydrogen in a separate vessel according to the reaction:

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The equipment for this experiment consists of a 36-liter feed tank, a $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ absorption vessel, a $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ reduction column, an effluent stream sampler, a 36-liter receiver, NaF traps for collecting excess $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ and disposing of HF, gas supplies for argon, hydrogen, and $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ , and means for analyzing the gas streams from the reaction vessels.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The experiment is operated by pressurizing the feed tank with argon in order to displace salt from the feed tank to the UF $_6$ absorption tank at rates from 50 to 300 cc/min.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The feed salt will be LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ (72-16-12 mole %) MSBR fuel carrier salt containing up to 0.3-mole % UF $_4$ .

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: Uranium hexafluoride flow rates from 60 to 360 scc/min, $^*$ and H $_2$ flow rates from 60 to 720 scc/min will be used.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The UF $_6$ absorption vessel will have an inside diameter of 4 in. and an inside height of 11 in.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The vessel will be constructed from 4-in. sched 40 nickel pipe mounted vertically with a 150-lb standard Monel flange at the top and a 0.25-in. nickel plate bottom.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: Two 1/2-in. nozzles provide for salt flow in and out of the vessel.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: Two 3/8-in. nozzles provide for UF $_6$ (g) flow in and off-gas flow out of the tank.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The $\mathsf{H}_2$ reduction column will have an overall height of 115 in.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: It is constructed of 1-1/2-in. sched 40 nickel pipe mounted vertically with a 150-lb Monel flange on the upper end, and a sched 40 nickel welded cap at the lower end.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: Gas enters through a 3/8-in. sched 40 nickel side arm at the bottom, while 1/2-in. and 3/8-in. nozzles provide for salt entrance and an off-gas exit.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: Because of the highly corrosive nature of dissolved $\mathsf{UF}_{5}$ , all equipment exposed to significant quantities of $\mathsf{UF}_{5}$ will be gold or gold plated after a start up and demonstration period using the equipment described in this report.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The 36-liter feed tank will have an inside diameter of 10 in. and an inside height of 33 in.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: It will be a nickel vessel with 0.25-in.-thick walls, equipped with two 1/2-in. nozzles for salt exit and entrance lines, two 3/8-in. nozzles for argon sparging and off-gas exit, and a 3/4-in. sampling port.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: All piping exposed to the liquid phase (MSBR carrier salt) or temperature in excess of $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ will be nickel pipe or tubing.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: Off-gas lines and liquid phase piping will be 1/2-in. tubing.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The UF $_6$ removal trap has a length of 55-5/8 in., while the HF trap has a length of 31-5/8 in.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The traps are constructed of 4-in. sched 40 Monel pipe mounted vertically with 150-lb standard flanges at both ends for charing and removing NaF pellets.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The gas enters from 1/2-in. nickel tubing through the flanged end and exits through the lower flanged end.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: A 6-in. section in the lower end of the trap contains 4-in.-diam Monel York mesh to keep pellets from plugging the exit.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The sampler is a vertically mounted 3/4-in. sched 40 pipe, 20-3/4 in. in length; it has a stainless steel full-bore valve located 12-1/4 in. from the lower end to allow access to the flowing salt stream.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The salt flows into the lower end of the sampler from 1/2-in. tubing and exits 1-1/4 in. from the end, again using 1/2-in. tubing.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: This assures a minimum of 1/4 in. of salt for insertion of a sampler.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: The sampler is a stainless steel 0.250-in. diam by 1-in.-long tube with a 0.050-in. fritted metal filter on the lower end.

section: 5. FUEL RECONSTITUTION DEVELOPMENT: DESIGN OF A FUEL RECONSTITUTION ENGINEERING EXPERIMENT
text: It is connected to a 1/16-in. stainless steel tube which in turn is attached to a vacuum pump.
